title: Insulating a rabbit hutch for winter
subtitle: Keeping outdoor rabbits warm without trapping moisture.
excerpt: Outdoor rabbits tolerate cold better than heat but suffer from draughts and damp. A well-insulated hutch keeps the sleeping area above 0°C in a UK winter without heating. The key is insulation that breathes, sealed polystyrene traps moisture and leads to the wet conditions rabbits are more vulnerable to than cold.
sourceNotes: RSPCA rabbit care guidelines, rspca.org.uk. British Rabbit Council housing guidance. RWAF (Rabbit Welfare Association and Fund) outdoor care advice.

glossaryTerms.definition: A fitted weatherproof cover placed over a rabbit hutch to reduce wind chill and rain penetration, typically made from waterproof fabric with ventilation panels.

content.text: Domestic rabbits kept outdoors year-round acclimatise to seasonal temperature changes if the transition is gradual. A rabbit that has lived outside all summer handles winter cold well. What it cannot tolerate is sudden cold, draughts, wet bedding, and the freeze-thaw cycle that damp hutch insulation causes.

Line the interior walls of the sleeping box with hardboard or thin ply to create a dead air gap between the board and the outer hutch wall. A 20 mm gap is enough to raise the interior temperature by several degrees in cold weather. Do not use polystyrene sheet directly inside the hutch, rabbits chew it and the ingested fragments cause gut blockages.

Pack the sleeping area with a deep layer of hay (15 to 20 cm). Hay insulates better than straw and the rabbit will burrow into it. Change the hay every three to four days in winter, damp hay loses its insulating value and encourages respiratory infections.

reduces wind chill significantly. Roll it down over the mesh front at night and lift it during the day to allow light and ventilation. The cover must not be sealed, a completely enclosed hutch becomes humid as the rabbit breathes and produces urine, which is harder on the respiratory system than the cold outside.

Cover the roof with a piece of waterproof roofing felt or a tarpaulin to stop water dripping through. Many hutch roofs deteriorate after two or three seasons and allow rain to penetrate, which soaks the insulation from above. Check the roof at the start of each autumn.

Move the hutch to a sheltered spot for winter if it is currently in an open position, beside a wall, under a lean-to, or in an outbuilding. The prevailing wind direction matters more than compass orientation. A hutch with its mesh front facing into the prevailing wind in November will be significantly colder inside than one turned away.

Water bottles freeze solid in sub-zero temperatures. Switch to a heavy ceramic bowl that takes longer to freeze, or check the bottle twice daily and replace it with warm water in the morning. A bottle with ice in it means the rabbit may not have drunk for hours.

A rabbit that is reluctant to leave the sleeping area, sitting huddled, or showing reduced feed intake in cold weather may be too cold. Feel the inside of the sleeping box, it should be noticeably warmer than the outside temperature, not merely the same. If the bedding feels damp, increase insulation and improve ventilation in equal measure.
